Kodak's alternatives should have been evaluated against a scenario where digital adoption was fast, a scenario where it was slow, and a scenario where a new competitor (camera phones) disrupted both film and digital cameras.

The MECE principle: alternatives should be Mutually Exclusive (no overlap) and Collectively Exhaustive (covering all reasonable options). A MECE alternative set for Kodak might have been: (A) defensive film: maximize film profitability while winding down, harvest cash; (B) digital pivot: aggressively shift resources to digital cameras and photo printing; (C) platform play: use photo storage and sharing as the core business; (D) exit: sell or liquidate. These four cover the option space without overlap.

How this connects to every business function: alternative generation is a cross-functional activity — the marketing team may generate alternatives focused on customer value; operations may generate alternatives focused on cost and feasibility; finance may generate alternatives focused on capital efficiency; HR may generate alternatives focused on capability. Bringing multiple functional perspectives to alternative generation expands the option space more than any single function can achieve alone.
learn.mechanismSummary: Alternatives must be generated expansively before evaluation — brainstorming, forced analogies, extreme scenarios, and MECE structuring overcome cognitive biases that narrow the option space prematurely.
learn.boundaryConditions: Alternative generation has diminishing returns — at some point, additional alternatives add noise rather than signal. The goal is a set of genuinely different approaches (different strategies, not variations on one strategy) that covers the option space, not an exhaustive list of every conceivable option. Additionally, generating alternatives is only useful if evaluation follows rigorously — an organization that generates many alternatives but always chooses the familiar one hasn't actually expanded its decision-making.
learn.example: When Apple considered entering the music business in 2001, the options they considered included: (1) build a digital download store, (2) license their existing software to music labels, (3) ignore digital music and focus on computers, (4) partner with an existing music service (Napster was in legal trouble; other services existed), or (5) create an integrated hardware-software ecosystem (iPod + iTunes + iTunes Store) that controlled the entire music experience. Options 1-4 were all being considered by competitors. Option 5 was genuinely different and required generating the insight that the music industry's real problem was a trusted payment mechanism and a legal path to individual song downloads — not just a better player. The alternative that proved transformative was not an obvious variation on existing options.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Generating alternatives is the step that determines the ceiling on decision quality — you cannot choose a better alternative than the best one you generated. The systematic biases that narrow alternative generation (anchoring, satisficing, organizational politics) operate in every organization, which means deliberate techniques to counteract them produce better decisions across every function and every decision type. This applies to product alternatives (idea-to-market), supplier alternatives (procurement), strategic alternatives (growth strategy), and organizational alternatives (restructuring) — anywhere choices must be made.
keyPoints: Anchoring on the first alternative limits the search — every alternative generated after the first is implicitly compared to it rather than evaluated on its own merits; generating multiple alternatives before evaluating any of them avoids this bias

MECE alternative structuring (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive) reveals gaps in the option space — if the alternatives listed don't cover all reasonable approaches, important options are being excluded rather than evaluated and rejected

Cross-functional alternative generation consistently produces better option sets — marketing, operations, finance, and HR each see different alternatives from their functional vantage; the best decision-making teams combine these perspectives before evaluating any single option

learn.openingQuestion: In 2019, WeWork was valued at $47 billion, preparing for an IPO, and its CEO Adam Neumann was on the cover of business magazines. Six months later, Neumann was forced out, the IPO was withdrawn, and the company nearly collapsed. Dozens of sophisticated investors had evaluated WeWork and concluded it was worth $47 billion. What analytical failures produced that evaluation — and what would a rigorous evaluation have found?
learn.anchor: Evaluating alternatives requires applying consistent criteria — financial viability, strategic fit, risk, and implementation feasibility — to each option and being willing to reject options that look attractive on one dimension but fail on others.
learn.anchorSummary: Alternative evaluation applies decision criteria systematically — financial analysis, risk assessment, strategic fit, and feasibility — against each alternative; the goal is to find the best option, not to confirm the initial preference.
learn.mechanism:
Evaluation is where case analysis (and real business decision-making) most commonly fails — not because the tools are wrong but because cognitive biases and incentive pressures corrupt their application. The WeWork analysis was distorted by: herd behavior (SoftBank's massive investment convinced other investors the valuation was credible), anchoring (each investor anchored to the previous round's valuation), narrative seduction (Neumann's charismatic story about 'elevating consciousness' distracted from WeWork's simple economics — it was a real estate company with long-term leases and short-term customers), and confirmation bias (investors who had already committed to the deal evaluated new information to confirm the investment rather than to honestly test it).

A rigorous evaluation framework:

1. Define criteria before evaluating: establish the criteria for a good alternative (and their relative weights) before examining how each alternative scores. Pre-defined criteria resist post-hoc rationalization. For WeWork: unit economics (is the margin positive at the unit level?), leverage (what happens if occupancy drops 20%?), competitive moat (why can't a competitor with the same leases do this cheaper?), and management quality.

2. Financial analysis: what are the revenue projections, cost structure, and expected returns for each alternative? Financial modeling should test the base case and scenarios (what if demand is lower? what if costs are higher?). Sensitivity analysis reveals which assumptions most affect the outcome — those are the critical uncertainties to focus research on. WeWork's financial analysis would have shown: occupancy risk (long-term leases with short-term members creates massive leverage to occupancy declines), negative unit economics at most locations (losing money per desk rented), and no competitive moat (IWG/Regus had been doing the same business profitably for 30 years at scale).

3. Risk assessment: for each alternative, what are the key risks? How likely are they? How severe? Can they be mitigated? Risk assessment is not a list of things that could go wrong — it is a prioritized analysis of the most consequential uncertainties and what each alternative's exposure to them is.

4. Strategic fit: does this alternative align with the organization's core competencies, mission, and strategic direction? An alternative can be financially attractive but require capabilities the organization doesn't have and can't develop.

5. Implementation feasibility: can this alternative actually be executed? Do we have the talent, the systems, the organizational structure, and the time to execute it? A brilliant strategy that cannot be implemented is not a viable alternative.

6. Comparison matrix: organize each alternative's performance on each criterion in a matrix. No alternative is best on all criteria — the matrix makes the trade-offs explicit and visible, enabling an informed choice rather than an intuitive one.

How this connects to every business function: evaluation draws on functional expertise — finance for financial modeling, operations for feasibility assessment, marketing for market size estimation, HR for implementation capability, and strategy for competitive fit. Multi-functional evaluation teams consistently produce more complete evaluations than single-function teams.
learn.mechanismSummary: Effective evaluation: pre-define criteria → financial analysis with scenarios → risk assessment → strategic fit → implementation feasibility → comparison matrix — the goal is to expose trade-offs, not to confirm a preferred alternative.
learn.boundaryConditions: Evaluation is only as good as the assumptions underlying the financial models. WeWork's models assumed high occupancy and continued revenue growth — reasonable assumptions in a bull market but catastrophically optimistic in an economic downturn. All financial models require explicit sensitivity analysis: what happens if the most important assumption is 20% worse than expected? If the answer is 'the alternative no longer makes sense,' the alternative's viability depends on the assumption being correct — which is a risk, not a certainty.
learn.example: Amazon's evaluation of the Whole Foods acquisition in 2017 was rigorous precisely because Amazon has experience buying physical retail assets. The evaluation would have included: Whole Foods' unit-level economics (profitable at store level), real estate portfolio (owned vs. leased, location quality), supply chain (direct relationships with local and organic producers), brand (the premium organic brand had genuine customer loyalty), and integration feasibility (Amazon had spent years building grocery delivery capability that lacked physical presence). The $13.7 billion price was premium but defensible against the strategic criteria: physical store presence, premium brand entry, and supply chain relationships that would have taken years and billions to build organically. Not all of Amazon's acquisition evaluations have been as successful — its acquisition of MGM and Zappos had mixed results — but the Whole Foods evaluation matched the strategic criteria to the financial price coherently.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Evaluating alternatives is where rigorous thinking and cognitive discipline intersect. The tools (financial modeling, sensitivity analysis, risk assessment, comparison matrices) are not complex — their rigorous application is. The most common failure is evaluating alternatives to confirm a preference rather than to find the best option. Developing the discipline to apply evaluation criteria consistently — including to alternatives you favor — is the core analytical skill that distinguishes excellent business decision-makers from merely intelligent ones.
keyPoints: Define criteria before evaluating alternatives — post-hoc criteria selection (choosing criteria that make the preferred alternative look best) is rationalization, not analysis; pre-defined criteria resist this bias

Sensitivity analysis is the most important financial evaluation tool — identifying which assumptions most affect the outcome reveals where the analysis is most vulnerable and where to focus additional research

Cross-functional evaluation consistently outperforms single-function evaluation: finance identifies economic viability, operations identifies feasibility, marketing identifies market size, HR identifies implementation capability — excluding any function produces an incomplete evaluation

learn.openingQuestion: A consulting team at McKinsey spends eight weeks analyzing a client's supply chain problem. They have 3,000 slides of analysis, 47 potential recommendations, and a meeting with the CEO in 45 minutes. The CEO's first question is: 'So what should we do?' What does the McKinsey team say?
learn.anchor: A recommendation is a clear, specific, defensible statement of what should be done, why, and in what priority order — it synthesizes analysis into action, not just into more analysis.
learn.anchorSummary: A recommendation specifies what to do, why, in what order, and acknowledges trade-offs — it synthesizes all prior analysis into a clear directive, not a summary of complexity.
learn.mechanism:
The McKinsey team's 45-minute meeting collapses three problems into one: what do we recommend? why do we recommend it? how do we communicate it in the time available? Great recommendations solve all three simultaneously.

A high-quality recommendation has five characteristics:

1. Specific and actionable: 'improve supply chain performance' is not a recommendation — it is a direction. 'Consolidate from three distribution centers to two, closing the Nashville facility, and renegotiate carrier contracts by Q3' is a recommendation. The decision-maker should be able to begin implementation immediately.

2. Grounded in analysis: the recommendation must be traceable to the analysis — not just to the recommender's intuition. 'Based on the unit economics analysis showing negative contribution margin at the Nashville facility and 15% excess capacity at Atlanta, closure saves $4.2M annually with a payback period of 18 months' grounds the recommendation in the evidence.

3. Acknowledges trade-offs: no alternative is best on every dimension. An honest recommendation acknowledges what is sacrificed by the recommended choice: 'The Nashville closure improves margin but reduces delivery speed to Tennessee customers by 1 day — we recommend accepting this trade-off because fewer than 12% of Nashville-zone orders are time-sensitive.'

4. Addresses risk: what could go wrong with the recommended course, and what mitigations are in place? 'The primary risk is disruption during transition — we recommend a phased migration over 8 weeks rather than simultaneous closure to minimize service disruption.'

5. Prioritized if multiple: when multiple changes are recommended, they must be sequenced. Which comes first? What are the dependencies? 'We recommend three actions in this sequence: (1) immediately renegotiate carrier contracts (low cost, high return), (2) begin Nashville transition planning while contracts are renegotiated (parallel workstreams), (3) execute facility consolidation in Q3.'

The pyramid principle (Barbara Minto): structure the recommendation communication top-down — lead with the conclusion, then support it with the reasoning, then provide the evidence. Most business presenters do the opposite: evidence → reasoning → conclusion. The pyramid inverts this — decision-makers need the conclusion first to evaluate whether the reasoning and evidence are relevant.

The 'so what' test: every piece of analysis should answer 'so what does this mean for the recommendation?' Analysis that doesn't connect to the recommendation is interesting but not relevant to the decision. The McKinsey team's 3,000 slides contain the evidence; the CEO meeting needs only the slides that connect directly to the recommendation.

How this connects to every business function: making recommendations is a cross-functional skill — a supply chain recommendation has financial implications (cost), operational implications (implementation), HR implications (workforce effects), and marketing implications (customer service level). A recommendation that ignores any of these dimensions is incomplete. The recommender must integrate cross-functional consequences into both the analysis and the recommendation.
learn.mechanismSummary: Recommendations must be specific, grounded in analysis, acknowledge trade-offs, address risk, and be prioritized if multiple — the pyramid principle (conclusion first, then support) is the communication structure that matches how decision-makers consume information.
learn.boundaryConditions: Recommendations are constrained by the decision-maker's authority and resources. A recommendation to 'restructure the entire organization' is not actionable for a department head with no authority over organizational design. Recommendations must be calibrated to what the decision-maker can actually do — not to what would be ideal in an unconstrained world. Additionally, the confidence with which a recommendation is stated should match the strength of the underlying evidence — overstating certainty on a weak analysis damages credibility more than acknowledging uncertainty.
learn.example: When Steve Jobs returned to Apple in 1997 and was asked by the board what to do with a company losing $1 billion per year, he could have presented a detailed analysis of every product line, every division, and every strategic option. Instead, his recommendation was startlingly simple: 'Apple needs to focus.' He eliminated 70% of Apple's product line within his first year — keeping only four products (two desktop computers, two portable computers). The recommendation was specific (which products to keep), grounded in his analysis (Apple had too many products to execute any of them well with constrained resources), acknowledged trade-offs (revenue from discontinued products would be lost in the short term), addressed risk (the fewer products strategy concentrated revenue risk), and was immediately actionable. The simplicity was not naive — it was the synthesis of a complex situation into the single most important lever.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Making a recommendation is the ultimate test of analytical rigor — it forces the decision-maker to commit to a specific course of action, accept the trade-offs it entails, and be accountable for the outcome. The process of case analysis (reading, problem definition, alternative generation, evaluation) is only valuable if it culminates in a clear, actionable recommendation. Business education teaches not just how to analyze but how to act on the analysis — translating insight into commitment.
keyPoints: Lead with the conclusion (pyramid principle): decision-makers need the recommendation first, then the rationale — building to the conclusion requires them to hold all the analysis in their head before they know what it's for

Acknowledge trade-offs explicitly: a recommendation that ignores what is sacrificed by the recommended choice is intellectually dishonest; acknowledging trade-offs demonstrates that the recommender understands the full decision space

Recommendations must be calibrated to the decision-maker's authority and resources — a brilliant recommendation that requires authority or budget the decision-maker doesn't have is not a recommendation; it is a wish

learn.openingQuestion: Richard Feynman, the Nobel Prize-winning physicist, said: 'If you can't explain something simply, you don't understand it well enough.' If a surgeon can explain a complex operation to a patient in plain language, a lawyer can explain a complicated case to a jury, and a teacher can explain calculus to a 17-year-old — why do so many business presentations fail to communicate analysis clearly to executives who already understand business?
learn.anchor: Communicating a case analysis effectively requires matching the structure, depth, and language of the communication to the audience's needs and the decision they must make — not to the completeness of the analysis.
learn.anchorSummary: Effective business communication is audience-centered: lead with the recommendation, support with structured reasoning, use evidence selectively, and adapt format and depth to what the audience needs to decide.
learn.mechanism:
Business communication is a distinct skill from business analysis. You can perform excellent analysis and communicate it poorly; you can structure a compelling presentation around weak analysis. Both skills matter, but they are different.

The pyramid principle for structure: every business communication should be organized as follows:
- Top: the governing recommendation or conclusion (what is the answer?)
- Second level: the key supporting arguments (why is this the right answer?)
- Third level: the evidence supporting each argument (what proves the argument?)

This top-down structure serves decision-makers who need the conclusion first to know how to interpret the evidence. A bottom-up structure (evidence → analysis → conclusion) forces the audience to hold all the evidence in working memory before they know what it proves — inefficient and cognitively demanding.

Situation-Complication-Resolution (SCR) framework: a simple three-part structure for any business communication:
- Situation: what is the current state? (Shared context the audience already knows, briefly.)
- Complication: what has changed or what tension exists that makes a decision necessary?
- Resolution: the recommendation that resolves the complication.

Audience calibration:
- Executives (CEO, board): summary recommendation upfront, three key supporting arguments, one or two pages maximum. They will ask questions if they want more detail — have the detail ready but don't present it unless asked.
- Managers: recommendation plus process and implementation plan. They need to understand not just what to do but how to make it happen.
- Technical teams: full analysis, methodology, assumptions, sensitivity testing. They need to evaluate the analysis on its merits and implement it with precision.

Visual communication: use visuals to communicate what visuals communicate better than text — trends (line charts), comparisons (bar charts), proportions (pie or stacked bar), and relationships (scatter plots). A slide with a paragraph of text is not a visual — it is a text document formatted as a slide. 'So what?' headlines: every slide should have a headline that states the insight or conclusion the slide supports, not a description of its content. 'Revenue by Region' is a descriptive headline. 'North America Revenue Declining While Asia Grows 40%' is an insight headline that supports a recommendation.

Handling uncertainty and disagreement: state clearly what you know, what you assume, and what you don't know. Presenting analysis with false certainty damages credibility when challenged. 'Our base case assumes 5% market growth; if growth is 2%, the recommendation changes to X' demonstrates analytical rigor. When presenting to stakeholders who may disagree, acknowledge the strongest counter-argument before presenting the recommendation — it demonstrates that you have considered alternatives seriously.

How this connects to every business function: every function must communicate its analysis and recommendations to stakeholders — operations teams communicating efficiency analyses to finance, marketing teams communicating campaign results to executives, HR teams communicating people analytics to business leaders. The communication skill is the final step that determines whether excellent analysis produces organizational action.
learn.mechanismSummary: Pyramid structure (conclusion → arguments → evidence), SCR framework (situation → complication → resolution), insight headlines, and audience calibration are the tools of effective business communication — structure serves the audience's decision, not the analyst's process.
learn.boundaryConditions: Communication effectiveness depends partly on organizational culture and norms — Amazon's six-page memo format, McKinsey's PowerPoint-heavy presentations, and tech companies' data-driven slide culture all represent different communication conventions that outsiders must adapt to. Additionally, in high-stakes or politically sensitive situations, the sequencing and framing of communication requires judgment beyond any framework — knowing when to lead with the conclusion vs. when to build context depends on reading the specific stakeholder relationship.
learn.example: Jeff Bezos's six-page narrative memo requirement at Amazon is a deliberate communication design choice based on the insight that PowerPoint presentations allow speakers to obscure weak thinking behind compelling visuals. A six-page narrative memo requires the author to articulate complete sentences, logical connections between ideas, and explicit acknowledgment of assumptions and trade-offs. The memo is read silently at the start of the meeting — eliminating the presentation performance from the evaluation. Bezos is testing the quality of thinking, not the quality of presenting. The communication format (narrative memo read silently) was designed to surface analytical weakness that PowerPoint slides can hide — a deliberate inversion of the standard business communication format.
learn.bridgeToAbstract: Communicating a case analysis is the final step in the business problem-solving cycle — it is where excellent analysis either produces organizational action or gets lost in unclear communication. Every skill developed across MIE 201 culminates in this step: understanding what business is (Unit 1), how it is organized (Unit 2), how it creates value through entrepreneurship and innovation (Unit 3), how it manages money (Units 4-5), how it reaches customers (Units 6-7), how it operates (Units 8-9), how it is managed (Units 10-11), how it competes strategically (Unit 12), what technology enables (Unit 13), what ethics and law require (Unit 14), how it operates globally (Unit 15), how its processes connect (Unit 16), and how it measures performance (Unit 17) — all of this knowledge is mobilized in the case analysis process, and communicated in a recommendation that enables a decision. That is the whole game.
keyPoints: The pyramid principle inverts the natural analytical sequence: present the conclusion first, then the arguments, then the evidence — this matches how executives consume information, not how analysts develop it

Insight headlines ('North America Revenue Declining While Asia Grows 40%') communicate more value than descriptive ones ('Revenue by Region') — every slide should answer 'so what?' rather than 'what is this?'

Communication skill is what converts excellent analysis into organizational action — analysis that is not communicated effectively produces no decisions, no changes, and no value; the communication is the final step that determines whether all the prior analytical work was worthwhile
